sections.title: Cash Basis vs Accrual Basis
sections.content:
**Cash basis** records revenues when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid.

**Accrual basis** records revenues when EARNED and expenses when INCURRED, regardless of cash flow.

SUSS and most businesses use accrual accounting because it provides a more accurate picture of financial performance during a period. Cash basis can be misleading if payment timing differs from when value is exchanged.

sections.title: The Revenue Recognition Principle
sections.content:
Revenue is recognised (recorded) when it is **earned** — when the service is performed or goods are delivered — not when cash is received.

**Example:** A consulting firm performs $10,000 of work in December but receives payment in January.
- Accrual basis: Record $10,000 revenue in December (when earned)
- Cash basis: Record $10,000 revenue in January (when cash received)

Accrual accounting assigns the revenue to the correct period.

sections.title: The Matching Principle
sections.content:
Expenses must be matched to the period in which they help generate revenue.

**Example:** A company uses electricity throughout December but receives the bill and pays in January.
- Accrual basis: Record the expense in December (when consumed)
- Cash basis: Record the expense in January (when paid)

Matching ensures profit is calculated fairly by pairing revenues with the expenses incurred to generate them.

sections.title: Prepayments (Prepaid Expenses)
sections.content:
A prepayment occurs when cash is paid **before** the expense is incurred.

**Example:** Paying 12 months of insurance $12,000 in January.
- Initial entry (January): Prepaid Insurance (Asset) **increases** $12,000; Cash (Asset) **decreases** $12,000
- Monthly adjusting entry (Jan–Dec): Insurance Expense **increases** $1,000; Prepaid Insurance (Asset) **decreases** $1,000

The prepaid asset is gradually expensed as the benefit is consumed. At period end, prepaid = unexpired portion.

sections.title: Accrued Expenses (Expenses Payable)
sections.content:
An accrued expense is incurred **but not yet paid**.

**Example:** December salaries $10,000 are earned by employees in December but paid in January.
- December adjusting entry: Salary Expense **increases** $10,000; Salaries Payable (Liability) **increases** $10,000
- January payment: Salaries Payable (Liability) **decreases** $10,000; Cash (Asset) **decreases** $10,000

Accruing ensures the expense is recorded in the period incurred, not when payment is made.

sections.title: Identify Accrual vs Prepayment
sections.content: Test your ability to distinguish between prepayments and accruals.

question.prompt: A company pays 6 months rent ($6,000) on 1 October. By 31 December, what adjustment is needed?
choices: Record $3,000 as expense and $3,000 as prepaid asset
question.explanation: Three months have passed (October, November, December), so $3,000 is expense. $3,000 remains prepaid for January-March. The prepayment must be split.

sections.title: Accrued Revenue (Revenue Receivable)
sections.content:
Revenue is earned **but cash has not yet been received**.

**Example:** A service firm completes a $5,000 project in December but invoices the customer in January.
- December adjusting entry: Accounts Receivable (Asset) **increases** $5,000; Service Revenue **increases** $5,000
- January collection: Cash (Asset) **increases** $5,000; Accounts Receivable (Asset) **decreases** $5,000

Accruing revenue ensures it is recorded in the period earned, maintaining matching principle.

sections.title: Unearned Revenue (Deferred Revenue)
sections.content:
Cash is received **before** the service is performed. This is a **LIABILITY**, not revenue, until the service is delivered.

**Example:** A customer pays $5,000 deposit in November for work to be done in December.
- November entry: Cash (Asset) **increases** $5,000; Unearned Revenue (Liability) **increases** $5,000
- December entry (on completion): Unearned Revenue (Liability) **decreases** $5,000; Service Revenue **increases** $5,000

Unearned revenue represents an obligation to perform — it is not income until earned.

sections.title: Unearned Revenue Treatment
sections.content: Understand how to handle customer advances.

question.prompt: A client pays $3,000 in advance for 3 months of consulting (January-March). At 31 January, what does the balance sheet show for the remaining advance?
question.explanation: $1,000 is earned in January and becomes revenue. $2,000 is still owed as a service (liability — unearned revenue). Liabilities appear on the balance sheet.

sections.title: Inventory and Receivables Treatments
sections.content:
**Inventory:** Recorded at cost, adjusted downward if obsolete or damaged (Lower of Cost or Market principle). Ensures assets are not overstated.

This is an example of the matching principle: assets are valued conservatively, and expenses are matched to revenues in the period they are incurred.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between accrual and cash basis accounting, and give one example of each.
questions.modelAnswer: Accrual basis records revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flow. Cash basis only records when cash moves in or out. Example accrual: December consulting revenue recorded even if payment arrives in January. Example cash basis: same revenue recorded only in January when cash received. Accrual gives a more accurate picture of performance in each period.
keyPoints: Accrual matches revenues and expenses to correct period

questions.prompt: A company pays 12 months insurance of $24,000 on 1 July. The financial year ends 31 December. What is the accounting treatment?
questions.modelAnswer: On 1 July: Prepaid Insurance (Asset) increases $24,000; Cash (Asset) decreases $24,000 (record the prepayment). By 31 December (6 months used): Insurance Expense increases $12,000; Prepaid Insurance (Asset) decreases $12,000 (adjusting entry). Balance sheet at 31 Dec shows Prepaid Insurance (asset) $12,000. Income statement shows Insurance Expense $12,000. The remaining 6 months stay as an asset.

questions.prompt: What is unearned revenue and how should it be treated on the balance sheet?
questions.modelAnswer: Unearned revenue is cash received before a service is performed or goods delivered. It is a LIABILITY because the company still owes the customer the service. It appears under current liabilities on the balance sheet (if expected to be satisfied within a year). As the service is performed, it transfers from liability to revenue through an adjusting entry.
keyPoints: Transferred to Revenue as service completed
